content:
===center

![explore1](https://cdna.artstation.com/p/assets/images/images/068/913/374/large/constantin-marin-bygonemarvels-constantinmarin.jpg?1698921656)

*Just imagine what kind of secrets this place holds*

===endcenter

===accordion
===panel:Introduction & Gameplan

## Introduction & Gameplan

Simic commanders really do need to do something fun/interesting to stand out these days.  Amongst the several variations of *"Draw cards"* and *"Play all the lands"*, [[Hakbal of the Surging Soul]] does so in a very creative way.

The Explore mechanic allows creatures to peak at the top card of our deck and if it's a land, we have to put it in our hand.  If it isn't, however, we have the choice to leave it on top or dump it into the graveyard.  It's that choice that makes Hakbal and his merry band of Merfolk Explorers such a compelling build-around.

Let's take a moment to break down [[Hakbal of the Surging Soul]] line by line:

***"At the beginning of combat on your turn, each Merfolk creature you control Explores."***

- To Explore, we reveal the top card of our library to everyone.  If it's a land, we must put it into our hand and that's the end of it.  However, if it's a non-land, we have the option to put it back on top or into the graveyard.  Regardless of what we choose here, the creature that explored receives a +1/+1 counter.

- Notice that this ability triggers at the beginning of combat, meaning we don't have to swing with any Merfolk to explore.  Hakbal makes it happen before we've attacked, which is a nice way of getting some value out of him on the same turn we play him, no need for Haste.

- Also note that the ability is tied only to Hakbal; he is making all Merfolk explore rather than giving the ability to them.  So an opponent could, for example [[Disallow]] when Hakbal triggers and that would stop all of our exploration.  Adding onto that, because it is a single trigger, we have to determine the order in which our Merfolk will be exploring and lock it in.

- And because it's a single trigger, it means all Merfolk must finish Exploring before any other spells abilities can resolve.  This means that if we have [[Nicanzil, Current Conductor]], for example, she will indeed be triggering due to all those explorations but we don't resolve her triggers until after all of Hakbal's crew have finished their exploration.

- For more context, take a look at Hakbal's rulings on the [official Gatherer page](https://gatherer.wizards.com/Pages/Card/Details.aspx?multiverseid=640007).

***"Whenever [[Hakbal of the Surging Soul]] attacks, you may put a land card from your hand onto the battlefield.  If you don't, draw a card."***

- And there's the generic Simic value engine line.  I was almost worried they wouldn't give him one!

- Jokes aside, this is a really good way of ramping if needed or refilling our hand if needed.  Note that this ability works nicely with all of the Exploring Hakbal and his Merfolk crew will be doing.  If we keep exploring and dumping cards we don't like until we see one we really want (for example, [[Inspiring Call]] for board protection), we can leave that one on top, knowing we'll draw it when we attack with Hakbal.  Top deck manipulation is very strong here and helps us keep control of the board.

This deck is packed full of Merfolk cards with synergistic abilities hand-picked around swarming the board to get several Explore triggers off of Hakbal, with a light sprinkling of +1/+1 counter support because that is going to be our main win-con: beating opponents down with evasive Merfolk, muscles bulging thanks to their exploration efforts.

With all of that in mind, here is the general gameplan for this build:

===accordion
===panel:Early Game

Ramp into Hakbal as quickly as possible.  He's one of the few tribal commanders out there who can provide value on his own, but if possible, get some Merfolk into play beforehand so you get maximum value out of his explore trigger on the turn you play him.

In the early game, it's up to you if you want to be dumping your non-land cards off of the explore triggers.  I usually like to dig for more Merfolk to put on board so that my exploration gets stronger.  If I have a decent number of Merfolk already, I prefer to dig for some source of card draw (such as [[Bred for the Hunt]]) or board protection (such as [[Heroic Intervention]]).

Regardless of what you decide to do with your Explore triggers, your creatures will be getting buffed with +1/+1 counters and that's kind of the whole point.

Moving onto [[Hakbal of the Surging Soul]]'s second ability - the one that triggers when he attacks - I like to ramp in the early game, meaning I choose the "put a land into play" option.  However, if you've got a really good card on top of your deck (you should know after all of the Explore triggers), feel free to draw it instead.  I usually favor the ramp in the early game so that I can accelerate into my bigger, splashier spells more quickly or re-cast Hakbal in case he gets removed.

===endpanel
===panel:Mid Game

As you transition to the mid game, you should start getting more picky with your Explore triggers.  If you don't find a useful card on top, dump it without hesitation.

By "useful cards", at this stage of the game you should be keeping only cards in the following categories:

- **Insurance** - protection such as counter spells ([[Swan Song]], [[Fierce Guardianship]], etc.) or things like [[Heroic Intervention]] and [[Inspiring Call]].

- **Finishers** - anything that will let your Merfolk attack unhindered or force damage through blockers (such as [[Herald of Secret Streams]], [[Mist Dancer]], etc.).

- **Card Draw/Looting** - cards that will help you dig to find those win-cons faster (such as [[Cold-Eyed Selkie]], [[Benthic Biomancer]], etc.).

In other words, whenever you explore, try to keep the cards that will save your board from opponents' interaction or the ones that will help you close out the game.  If, however, you've missed a land drop, by all means dump every non-land card until one of your Explore triggers nets you a land into hand.

There's also another option to consider - if you've got a finisher lined up, you can keep a non-land on top and make sure all of your Merfolk receive a +1/+1 counter no matter what.  Let's say for example you have a [[Herald of Secret Streams]] in hand that you can cast next turn and a couple of Merfolk on the board with Hakbal but you don't have *quite* enough power to finish your opponents off.  You can move to explore and as soon as you get a non-land on top, you can leave it there so that the remainder of your explorers are guaranteed to receive a +1/+1 counter.  It's a niche situation but it can come up.  So remember if you absolutely need to guarantee those +1/+1 counters then be sure to leave a non-land card on top no matter how not-useful it is.

===endpanel
===panel:Late Game

It's not a very complicated strategy.  Buff our Merfolk and turn them sideways.  We have a few key effects that can help them attack unhindered:

- [[Lord of Atlantis|SPG:1]] and [[Master of the Pearl Trident]] make any opponent with an Island easy to bully.

- [[Herald of Secret Streams]], [[Deepchannel Mentor]], [[Merfolk Sovereign]] and [[Mist Dancer]] can make our Merfolk difficult or impossible to block.

- [[Zegana, Utopian Speaker]] and [[Sphere Grid]] grant our creatures with +1/+1 Trample so even if they are blocked, they're still pushing damage through.

Or something like [[Wanderwine Prophets]] which can let us take extra turns with enough Merfolk sac fodder to just beat everyone down before they have a chance to respond.

And of course, do not forget to track commander damage dealt by [[Hakbal of the Surging Soul]].  He's going to be buffing himself, so it's not uncommon for him to just take somebody out with commander damage.  21 is all it takes.

===endpanel
===endaccordion

===center
![explore2](https://pbs.twimg.com/media/F9f1_ovaoAAQrxN?format=jpg&name=4096x4096)

*Forget uncharted waters, this is an entire uncharted world!*
===endcenter

===endpanel
===panel:Pros, Cons & Power Level

## Pros, Cons & Power Level

**✅ Pros**

- Hakbal acts as a sort-of Merfolk lord thanks to his Explore buffing ability, while also acting as a card draw and ramp engine.  He's just really good value in the command zone.

- This deck doesn't feel nearly as boring as most of the other Ramp + Card Draw Simic commanders since the Explore mechanic offers a choice.  Aggressively dump cards or keep one you like on top.

**❌ Cons**

- It's pretty reliant on its commander being on the board, which is why we have several pieces of instant-speed interaction to help protect Hakbal.  Without him, this deck feels like a mediocre Merfolk tribal deck.

- Convincing people who hate Simic to try this deck is going to be tough.  Hakbal looks just as boring as Aesi or Kinnan on paper but trust me, he's a lot more fun in my opinion.

**☢️ Power Level**

- This deck is high-power casual.  It wants to win by putting lots of +1/+1 counters on Merfolk and swinging at opponents.

- There is a "combo" but it requires several hoops to jump through, usually takes two turns to assemble and doesn't even lead to a win.  I wouldn't consider it a win-con; it's detailed in the primer just in case you manage to find the cards to assemble it.

- The only reason this deck is in Bracket 4 is due to [[Harbinger of the Seas]], which qualifies as mass land denial and automatically makes the deck a 4 with its inclusion alone.  If you remove it for something else, this deck is firmly a 3.

===endpanel
===panel:Packages

## Packages

===accordion
===panel:Ramp (14)

- [[Hakbal of the Surging Soul]] and [[Nicanzil, Current Conductor]] let us put lands from our hand onto the field.

- [[Rampant Growth]], [[Shared Roots]], [[Farseek]], [[Three Visits]] and [[Nature's Lore]] all get lands out of our deck and onto the field.

- [[Sol Ring]], [[Arcane Signet]], [[Thought Vessel]] and [[The Great Henge]] all produce mana on their own.

- [[Kiora's Follower]] can untap a permanent which can be a land or mana rock, essentially functioning as ramp.

- [[Stonybrook Banneret]] reduces the cost of our Merfolk spells.

- [[Burgeoning]] can help us get lands out of our hand when our opponents play theirs.

===endpanel
===panel:Card Draw & Card Advantage (18)

- [[Hakbal of the Surging Soul]] is our main source of card draw, allowing us to do so if we choose not to put a land into play when he attacks.

- [[Silvergill Adept]] and [[Zegana, Utopian Speaker]] replace themselves upon ETB by drawing us a card.

- [[Benthic Biomancer]] lets us loot whenever one or more +1/+1 counters are placed on it.

- [[Cold-Eyed Selkie]] draws us cards equal to the amount of combat damage it deals to an opponent.

- [[Seafloor Oracle]] draws us a card whenever any Merfolk deals combat damage to an opponent.

- [[Bred for the Hunt]] draws us a card whenever a creature with a +1/+1 deals damage to an opponent.

- [[Svyelun of Sea and Sky]] draws us a card when she attacks.

- [[Attuma, Atlantean Warlord]] draws us a card whenever our Merfolk attack an opponent (max 1 card draw per opponent attacked).

- [[Kumena, Tyrant of Orazca]], [[Sage of Fables]] and [[Waterlogged Grove]] have activated abilities that can draw us cards.

- [[Tatyova, Benthic Druid]] draws us a card whenever a land ETBs on our field.

- [[Inspiring Call]] is a one-time card draw effect.

- [[The Great Henge]] draws us a card when a non-token creature ETBs on our field.

- [[Thada Adel, Acquisitor]] is technically card advantage because she lets us steal and play an artifact card out of an opponent's deck.

- [[Emperor Mihail II]] and [[Realmwalker]] let us cast creatures off the top of our library, which isn't card draw but functionally serves as card advantage.

===endpanel
===panel:Removal & Interaction (18)

- [[Cyclonic Rift]] and [[Beast Within]] can get rid of permanents.

- [[Bridgeworks Battle]], [[Pongify]] and [[Rapid Hybridization]] can get rid of creatures.

- [[Thieving Skydiver]] doesn't explicitly remove an artifact but it does let us steal it, which is arguably better than removal.

- [[Flare of Denial]], [[An Offer You Can't Refuse]], [[Swan Song]], [[Stubborn Denial]] and [[Fierce Guardianship]] can all counter spells on the stack.

- [[Tishana's Tidebinder]] can counter an activated or triggered ability.

- [[Vodalian Hexcatcher]] lets us sacrifice Merfolk and force our opponents to pay more mana to have their non-creature spells resolve; if they can't pay, that spell gets countered.

- [[Heroic Intervention]] and [[Inspiring Call]] can be cast in response to removal and save our creature(s).

- [[Boseiju, Who Endures]] and [[Otawara, Soaring City]] both have channel abilities that can get rid of certain permanents.

- [[Singer of Swift Rivers]] isn't interaction in the traditional sense, but it can be cast at instant-speed and when it ETBs, it puts a Shield Counter on any of our creatures which can eat up any instance of damage or destruction, effectively negating an opponent's attempt at removing one of our creatures.

===endpanel
===panel:Counters Matter (14)

This section points out all of the cards in the deck that either generate +1/+1 counters or care about them in some way.

- [[Hakbal of the Surging Soul]] is our main source of buffing our Merfolk thanks to his Explore trigger on each of them.

- [[Topography Tracker]] and [[Roaming Throne|LCI:258]] let our Merfolk explore more than they otherwise would, allowing them to get more +1/+1 counters.

- [[Deeproot Elite]], [[Kumena, Tyrant of Orazca]], [[Sage of Fables]], [[The Great Henge]] and [[Court of Garenbrig]] can all place +1/+1 counters on our creatures.

- [[Herald of Secret Streams]] makes all of our creatures with +1/+1 counters completely unblockable.

- [[Zegana, Utopian Speaker]] and [[Sphere Grid]] grant our creatures with +1/+1 counters Trample, so they'll be dealing damage through blockers.

- [[Inspiring Call]] can save our creatures with +1/+1 counters and draw us cards as a bonus.

- [[Bred for the Hunt]] draws us a card whenever a creature with a +1/+1 deals damage to an opponent.

- [[Hardened Scales]] increases the amount of +1/+1 counters our creatures receive from each effect.

===endpanel
===panel:Evasion & Finishers (15)

- [[Hakbal of the Surging Soul]] can easily take somebody out with enough hits thanks to commander damage.

- [[Lord of Atlantis|SPG:1]] and [[Master of the Pearl Trident]] can grant our Merfolk Islandwalk, making them unblockable if swinging at an opponent with an Island.  [[Tide Shaper]] can make this happen if needed.

- [[Cold-Eyed Selkie]] and [[Stonybrook Banneret]] have Islandwalk on their own.

- [[Merfolk Sovereign]], [[Herald of Secret Streams]], [[Mist Dancer]] and [[Deepchannel Mentor]] can make our creatures unblockable or evasive, allowing for easy combat damage.

- [[Kumena, Tyrant of Orazca]] can make himself unblockable.

- [[Thieving Skydiver]] has Flying, making it a serious threat if it's loaded up with +1/+1 counters.

- [[Zegana, Utopian Speaker]] and [[Sphere Grid]] grant our creatures with +1/+1 counters Trample, so they'll be dealing damage through blockers.

- [[Wanderwine Prophets]] can let us take multiple turns if we have expendable Merfolk to spare, allowing us to beat down our opponents without giving them a chance to fight back.

===endpanel
===endaccordion

===center
![explore3](https://draftsim.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/11/Silvergill-Adept-Illustration-by-Magali-Villeneuve.jpg)

*We leave no stone unturned*
===endcenter

===endpanel
===panel:Notable Includes

## Notable Includes

Certain cards in the deck warranted a "deeper dive" (I'll show myself out).  Click/tap each heading below to expand and read the panel.

===accordion|autocollapse
===panel:Benthic Biomancer

Just something to keep in mind: Hakbal's Explore trigger is mandatory, meaning all of our Merfolk must Explore when we move to combat.  If [[Benthic Biomancer]] explores a non-land card, he will receive a +1/+1 counter.  When that happens, we have to loot (draw + discard), this is also mandatory.

A lot of the time this really isn't a big deal but I wanted to point out for the sake of correct play that both the Exploring and the Looting are not optional.

===endpanel
===panel:Roaming Throne

[[Roaming Throne|LCI:258]] is phenomenal tribal support card that doubles up our triggered abilities of whatever creature type we name with it.

With [[Roaming Throne|LCI:258]] and [[Hakbal of the Surging Soul]] on board, each of our Merfolk will get to explore and then explore again.  But it gets better - if we have [[Topography Tracker]], we get to explore 4 times with each Merfolk.  The reason it's like this is because we get two separate Explore triggers on our combat step thanks to the Throne, and [[Topography Tracker]] applies its replacement effect to each one.  In other words - Explore twice, then Explore twice again.

It gets even more crazy if you happen to get [[Glasspool Mimic]] into play as a copy of [[Roaming Throne|LCI:258]].

Furthermore, Hakbal gets to use his second ability twice with [[Roaming Throne|LCI:258]], allowing us to put two lands into play, or draw two cards or do one of each.

And even beyond that, [[Roaming Throne|LCI:258]] assists several other Merfolk in the deck with their triggered abilities.  [[Silvergill Adept]] draws us two cards, [[Thieving Skydiver]] can steal two artifacts, [[Singer of Swift Rivers]] can protect two creatures.  [[Tide Shaper]] can turn two lands into Islands.  The value is nuts.

===endpanel
===panel:Tide Shaper

Speaking of [[Tide Shaper]], this is a great card for both offensive and disruptive purposes.

First off, it can turn any land into an Island.  And don't get this confused with something like [[Aquitect's Will]], which turns a land into and Island *in addition to its other types*, no, [[Tide Shaper]] straight up removes the land's other functionality.  [[Gaea's Cradle]], [[Cabal Coffers]], [[Nykthos, Shrine to Nyx]]?  No more massive mana production, one [[symbol:u]] is all you get.  [[Field of the Dead]]?  No zombies for you.  [[Inkmoth Nexus]]?  No infect shenanigans in this house!  [[Maze's End]]?  Find another win-con.

While this isn't strictly speaking removal, the ability to neuter a powerful land is a criminally underutilized in EDH.

Beyond that, [[Tide Shaper]] easily enables our Islandwalk so things like [[Cold-Eyed Selkie]] can attack someone who otherwise wouldn't have an Island.  [[Master of the Pearl Trident]] and [[Lord of Atlantis|SPG:1]] suddenly become way scarier to people playing decks without blue, thinking they're safe.

===endpanel
===panel:Wanderwine Prophets

[[Wanderwine Prophets]] is a great finisher in this deck if we have a large enough army of merfolk and are willing to sacrifice a few of them to take enough turns to KO everyone.

Because of the amount of Exploring and card draw we have, we shouldn't have too much trouble finding more merfolk to cast and create fodder for [[Wanderwine Prophets]] to keep taking extra turns.  [[Deeproot Pilgrimage]] and [[Deeproot Waters]] are fantastic for providing the sac fodder.

===endpanel
===panel:Explore Queue

Cards that give us knowledge of the top of our deck such as [[Emperor Mihail II]] and [[Realmwalker]] or Scry abilities (i.e. [[Path of Ancestry]]) can be used to our advantage when [[Hakbal of the Surging Soul]]'s explore trigger goes off at the beginning of combat.

Because Hakbal forcing the Merfolk squad to explore is a single trigger, we must determine the order in which our Merfolk explore and lock it in.  Now, let's say for example we want to buff Hakbal with a +1/+1 counter.  This won't happen if he explores and finds a land on top.  However, if we have knowledge of our top deck and know that there's a land on there, we can choose to make another Merfolk go first, knowing it will grab the land and hopefully afterward there will be a non-land so Hakbal gets that counter.

The reverse is also true - if we know there's a non-land on top, we can queue up Hakbal himself to go exploring first and guarantee that +1/+1 counter.

It's a minor bit of optimization but we want our big beaters to be receiving those +1/+1 counters while the merfolk support crew grab the lands.

===endpanel
===endaccordion

===endpanel
===panel:Combos

## Combos

===center
![jankycombo](https://i.imgur.com/8Fnse6j.jpg)
===endcenter

**Requirements**

- [[Kiora's Follower]] on the field (must be ready to tap and not summoning sick).

- [[Glasspool Mimic]] or [[Deepfathom Echo]] on the field as copy of [[Kiora's Follower]] (also must be ready to tap and not summoning sick).

- [[Deeproot Pilgrimage]] on the field.

**Steps**

1. Activate [[Kiora's Follower]] and untap any of your permanents (a land, for example).

2. This triggers [[Deeproot Pilgrimage]] and creates a Merfolk token.

3. Activate [[Glasspool Mimic]] targeting the original [[Kiora's Follower]] to untap it.

4. Repeat 1-3 for an infinite number of Merfolk tokens from [[Deeproot Pilgrimage]].

**Notes**

- Don't rely on this combo as a win-con because it's slow to assemble and execute.  Reason is that you need to get both [[Kiora's Follower]] and [[Glasspool Mimic]]/[[Deepfathom Echo]] into play but since they don't have haste and this deck doesn't have a way to grant haste, they'll have to sit there and pray they survive until next turn.  The other reason is that the Merfolk tokens you make if you manage to pull it off also don't have Haste so they won't win you the game on that turn either.

===endpanel
===panel:Upgrades & Alternate Options

## Upgrades & Alternate Options

===accordion
===panel:Upgrades

If you want to play this deck at higher-power tables, you can make a few upgrades, starting with even better interaction.  [[Force of Negation]] and [[Force of Will]] are basically free counter spells in a deck full of so many blue cards.  Feel free to add more interaction beyond that if you feel it's necessary, such as [[Mana Drain]], [[Pact of Negation]], etc.

[[Jeweled Lotus]] is another great include, since it basically means a turn 1 Hakbal.  Land drop + crack [[Jeweled Lotus]] for 3 mana of the other color and boom, start exploring right away.

After that you can look to add a combo.  One I considered was [[Benthic Biomancer]] + [[Wizard Class]] to loot through the entire deck followed by [[Thassa's Oracle]] for the win.

And finally, there's [[Simic Ascendancy]] which can grow extremely quickly thanks to so many Explore triggers this deck can create.  The trick is surviving the rotation around the table so you can win on the upkeep but an extra turn effect can get around that.  [[Wanderwine Prophets]] can get the job done.

===endpanel
===panel:Alternate Options

One last thing - if you're looking for a Merfolk tribal deck but Hakbal isn't quite your cup of tea, may I suggest taking a look at [Availence](https://www.moxfield.com/users/Avialence)'s primer on Kumena [here](https://www.moxfield.com/decks/WfiEYgbRUkKXjBDE46Ha_A).  It's a very fun Merfolk list built around untap effects to get maximum value out of [[Kumena, Tyrant of Orazca]].  I'd highly recommend giving it a try if Hakbal isn't doing it for you.

===endpanel
===endaccordion

===center
![cenotescout](https://mktg-assets.tcgplayer.com/fit-in/1100x1100/filters:quality(75)/content/opengraph/MTG-Cenote-Scout-LCI.png)

*The thrill of discovery outweighs any hint of fear!*
===endcenter

===endpanel
===panel:Budget Options

## Budget Options

**Lands**

- A good chunk of money can be saved by simply replacing the expensive dual lands with cheaper, budget alternatives that enter tapped.  Take a look through the list of [Simic Lands](https://managathering.com/duallands/simic.html) and make any replacements you need to make.  For example, [[Breeding Pool]] becomes [[Rimewood Falls]], etc.

- [[Boseiju, Who Endures]] and [[Otawara, Soaring City]] can be replaced with a basic [[Forest|LCI:291]] and [[Island|LCI:288]], respectively.

- [[Prismatic Vista]] → [[Island|LCI:288]]

- [[Yavimaya, Cradle of Growth]] → [[Forest|LCI:291]]

**Creatures**

- [[Roaming Throne|LCI:258]] → [[Merfolk Mistbinder]] or [[Coralhelm Commander]]

- [[Wanderwine Prophets]] → [[Tishana, Voice of Thunder]]

- [[Tishana's Tidebinder]] → [[Merrow Reejerey]]

**Instants**

- [[Swan Song]] → [[Miscast]] or [[Slip Out the Back]]

- [[Cyclonic Rift]] → [[Wave Goodbye]] or [[Filter Out]]

- [[Heroic Intervention]] → [[Ripples of Potential]]

- [[Fierce Guardianship]] → [[Counterspell]]

- [[Flare of Denial]] → [[Negate]]

**Artifacts**

- [[The Great Henge]] → [[Kindred Discovery]] or [[Herald's Horn]]

**Enchantments**

- [[Court of Garenbrig]] → [[Ozolith, the Shattered Spire]]

- [[Burgeoning]] → [[Merfolk Skydiver]]

===endpanel
===panel:Change Log

## Change Log

===accordion
===panel:6/1/24

- [[Agatha's Soul Cauldron]] is good but I wanted even more board protection so [[Flare of Denial]] was added in its place.

===endpanel
===panel:1/18/24

- Found myself not really using [[Merfolk Cave-Diver]] all that often since there are so many ways to push damage through with my other creatures.  Decided to replace it with [[Deepfathom Echo]] because I wanted to try it.

- Also found that I was drawing a ton of cards and having to dump them due to hand size so [[Thought Vessel]] was brought in and [[Talisman of Curiosity]] was removed.  [[Temple of Mystery]] was also removed in favor of [[Reliquary Tower]].

- Also the newly spoiled [[Hedge Maze]] is just objectively better than [[Tangled Islet]] so that was an easy swap.

===endpanel
===panel:1/9/24

- I typically prefer mana rocks over mana dorks but for this deck I made an exception: [[Fellwar Stone]] was replaced with [[Kiora's Follower]].

- [[Merfolk Skydiver]] was also replaced with [[Burgeoning]] because it was the weakest creature I could find in exchange for a bit more ramp.

===endpanel
===panel:12/21/23

- [[Guardian Project]] was replaced with [[Danny Pink]] because he costs the same amount of mana but has potential for more card draw overall.

===endpanel
===endaccordion

===endpanel
===endaccordion

===center
![explore4](https://i.imgur.com/lo5t2xX.jpg)

*Invaders ravaged the surface, but Ixalan has blessed us with a world beneath*
===endcenter
